skill.name: azd-deployment
skill.description: Deploy containerized applications to Azure Container Apps with Azure Developer CLI, azure.yaml, and Bicep infrastructure patterns.
skill.summary: Guide Azure Container Apps deployments with azd, Bicep, remote builds, hooks, and environment configuration.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: Static analysis correctly identified command, network, filesystem, and environment-access patterns, but the context is a markdown deployment guide rather than executable code. No prompt injection or malicious exfiltration intent was found. Publish with a medium-risk warning because the examples involve shell hooks, Azure role changes, and environment values.

medium_findings.title: Shell Hook Examples Execute Deployment Commands
medium_findings.description: The skill documents azd hooks that run shell commands during provisioning and deployment. This is legitimate for Azure deployments, but copied hooks can execute with cloud permissions and should be reviewed before use.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are inside documentation examples, not runnable package code. They are still real shell hook patterns that execute during azd workflows when users copy them.

medium_findings.title: Environment Value Handling Requires Secret Discipline
medium_findings.description: The guide references local .env files, azd-managed environment files, azd env set, and azd env get-values. These are normal azd features, but careless use can expose secrets in terminals, logs, or source control.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The skill explicitly discusses env files and commands that can display environment values. The guidance is deployment-related, but the operational risk is real if users mishandle secrets.

medium_findings.title: RBAC Assignment Examples Modify Cloud Access
medium_findings.description: The postprovision example creates Azure role assignments for managed identities. This is expected for Container Apps, but users must scope roles carefully to avoid excessive access.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The role assignment commands are visible and semantically valid Azure administration steps. The risk depends on the scopes users provide in their own environments.

low_findings.title: Static Command Execution Alerts Are Documentation Examples
low_findings.description: The analyzer flagged many command-like snippets as code execution. The file is a markdown guide with bash, YAML, Bicep, and nginx examples, not a script that automatically runs commands.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged commands appear inside fenced documentation blocks and require a user to copy and run them. No autonomous execution path was found in the skill files.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URLs Are Placeholder or Internal Service Examples
low_findings.description: The external URLs are Azure service endpoint examples, and the internal HTTP URL illustrates Container Apps service discovery. No evidence of data exfiltration endpoints was found.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs are clearly labeled as Azure endpoint examples or internal DNS references. They do not point to suspicious collection services.

low_findings.title: Temporary File Use Stores Deployment Metadata
low_findings.description: The hook example writes custom domain configuration to /tmp/domains.json and reads it later. This is a practical preservation pattern, but shared temp paths should be handled carefully.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The temporary file contains domain metadata, not credentials in the shown example. The risk is low but plausible if users adapt the pattern for sensitive values.

dangerous_patterns.title: azd Hooks Run Shell Commands
dangerous_patterns.description: The documented hooks run shell commands during provision and deploy phases. Users should treat copied hook bodies as privileged automation.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The hook structure and shell bodies are explicit in the guide. The skill itself does not execute them automatically.

dangerous_patterns.title: Environment Values Can Be Displayed or Persisted
dangerous_patterns.description: The guide includes azd environment files and commands that manage or display environment values. Secrets should remain out of source control and logs.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines directly discuss env files and environment commands. The risk is contextual because the examples are standard azd operations.

dangerous_patterns.title: Role Assignment Commands Change Azure Permissions
dangerous_patterns.description: The sample postprovision commands create Azure role assignments. They should be constrained to least-privilege scopes.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The Azure CLI role commands are explicit. They are legitimate but can broaden cloud access if copied with broad scopes.

content.user_title: Deploy Azure Container Apps with azd
content.value_statement: Container deployments to Azure can fail when configuration, infrastructure, and environment values are scattered. This skill provides practical azd and Bicep patterns for repeatable Container Apps deployments.
actual_capabilities: Explains the core azd project structure for Azure Container Apps.
Shows azure.yaml service settings for frontend and backend containers.
Describes Bicep parameter and output flow into azd environments.
Documents remote build settings for Azure Container Registry builds.
Provides hook patterns for custom domains and managed identity RBAC.
Lists common azd commands for deployment and troubleshooting.
limitations: It is documentation only and does not deploy resources by itself.
Users need an Azure subscription and the Azure Developer CLI.
Examples require review before use in production environments.
It focuses on Container Apps and does not cover every Azure host type.

use_cases.title: Create a new Container Apps deployment
use_cases.description: Plan the azd files, service layout, and remote build settings for a new containerized application.

use_cases.title: Standardize infrastructure configuration
use_cases.description: Map Bicep parameters, outputs, managed identities, and azd environment values into a repeatable deployment flow.

use_cases.title: Troubleshoot azd deployment behavior
use_cases.description: Review commands, environment flow, service discovery, and hook behavior when azd up fails or changes resources.

prompt_templates.title: Start an azd project
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me plan a basic azd project for one backend service on Azure Container Apps.

prompt_templates.title: Design service configuration
prompt_templates.prompt: Create an azure.yaml plan for a frontend and backend that use remote builds for Container Apps.

prompt_templates.title: Connect Bicep and environment values
prompt_templates.prompt: Explain how Bicep parameters and outputs should flow into azd environment values for my deployment.

prompt_templates.title: Review hooks and permissions
prompt_templates.prompt: Review my planned azd hooks for custom domains and managed identity RBAC before production use.

output_examples.input: I need to deploy a Python API to Azure Container Apps.
output_examples.output: The skill outlines the azd project files, service configuration, remote build choice, and deployment command sequence to prepare the API.

output_examples.input: My frontend needs to call the backend inside the same Container Apps environment.
output_examples.output: The skill explains internal service discovery and shows where the backend URL belongs in frontend environment settings.

output_examples.input: I need managed identity access to Azure OpenAI and AI Search.
output_examples.output: The skill describes enabling system-assigned identity, exporting the principal identifier, and assigning scoped RBAC roles after provisioning.

best_practices: Review every copied hook command before running azd up in a real subscription.
Store secrets with azd environment commands and keep env files out of source control.
Use least-privilege scopes for managed identity role assignments.
anti_patterns: Committing azd environment files that contain deployment values or secrets.
Using broad Azure scopes for role assignments copied from examples.
Running deployment hooks without checking their shell commands first.

faq.question: What does this skill help with?
faq.answer: It helps plan Azure Container Apps deployments using azd, azure.yaml, Bicep, remote builds, and deployment hooks.

faq.question: Does the skill run Azure commands automatically?
faq.answer: No. It is a markdown guide. Users must choose and run any commands themselves.

faq.question: Why is the audit risk medium?
faq.answer: The examples include shell hooks, environment values, and Azure role assignments. These are powerful when copied into real deployments.

faq.question: Can I use it with Claude Code or Codex?
faq.answer: Yes. It is compatible with Claude, Codex, and Claude Code workflows that read skill documentation.

faq.question: Does it include complete infrastructure files?
faq.answer: No. It provides patterns and examples that users adapt to their own repository and Azure resources.

faq.question: How should secrets be handled?
faq.answer: Use azd environment management and avoid committing environment files. Review terminal output before sharing logs.
